questionText: A curve is given parametrically by $x = \sin t$ and $y = \sin 2t$, for $0 \le t < 2\pi$. (a) Show that a Cartesian equation of the curve is $y^{2} = 4x^{2}\left(1 - x^{2}\right)$. (b) State the domain of $x$ and the range of $y$. (c) Find the coordinates of every point where the curve meets the coordinate axes.

steps.explanation: Here $y$ is a double angle of the same angle $t$ that appears in $x$. The double-angle formula $\sin 2t = 2\sin t\cos t$ will let us bring in $x = \sin t$ directly.
This is the standard expansion of $\sin 2t$. It contains both $\sin t$ and $\cos t$, so we will need each of them in terms of $x$.
Since $x = \sin t$, the $\sin t$ factor becomes $x$. The awkward part is the leftover $\cos t$, which is not directly one of our variables.

steps.description: Express cos t using the Pythagorean identity
steps.explanation: From $\sin^{2} t + \cos^{2} t = 1$ we get $\cos t = \pm\sqrt{1-x^{2}}$. The sign depends on which quarter of the circle $t$ lies in, so we must keep the $\pm$ for now.
This gives $y$ in terms of $x$, but the $\pm$ makes it a pair of equations rather than one clean curve.

steps.description: Remove the ambiguous sign by squaring
steps.explanation: Squaring both sides eliminates the troublesome $\pm$ and captures both branches in a single equation. This is the required Cartesian form.

steps.description: State the domain from x = sin t
steps.explanation: Because $x = \sin t$ and sine never leaves $[-1, 1]$, the curve only exists for $-1 \le x \le 1$.

steps.description: State the range from y = sin 2t
steps.explanation: Likewise $y = \sin 2t$ is a sine, so it too is bounded between $-1$ and $1$.

steps.description: Note the symmetry of the equation
steps.explanation: The equation only contains $x^{2}$ and $y^{2}$, so replacing $x$ by $-x$ or $y$ by $-y$ leaves it unchanged. The curve is therefore symmetric about both axes.
Setting $x=0$ forces $y=0$, so the only crossing on the $y$-axis is the origin.
On the $x$-axis $y=0$, so the right-hand side must vanish. A product is zero exactly when one of its factors is zero.
This gives $x = 0$ and $x = \pm 1$, all of which satisfy $-1 \le x \le 1$, so all are valid.
The curve passes through the origin and touches the $x$-axis at $(\pm 1, 0)$.
The two branches $y = \pm 2x\sqrt{1-x^{2}}$ join at the origin and at $(\pm 1, 0)$, producing a symmetric figure-of-eight made of two loops, one on each side of the $y$-axis.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: (a) $y^{2} = 4x^{2}\left(1 - x^{2}\right)$. (b) Domain $-1 \le x \le 1$, range $-1 \le y \le 1$. (c) The curve meets the axes at $(0,\,0)$, $(1,\,0)$ and $(-1,\,0)$; it is a symmetric figure-of-eight passing through the origin.
